headline: Bangladesh Makes History Releasing Rescued Tiger to the Wild
meta_description: After months of rehabilitation, a Bengal tiger rescued from a poacher's trap returns to the Sundarbans mangrove forest in a first for Bangladesh.
summary_short: A critically injured Bengal tiger rescued from a poacher's snare is being released back into Bangladesh's Sundarbans forest—the country's first such release.
body:
When the Bangladesh Forest Department pulled a female Bengal tiger from a deer poacher's metal snare in January 2026, the animal was in critical condition. Her left foreleg was severely damaged by the trap. Now, after months of care at the Khulna Wildlife Rescue and Rehabilitation Center, she is ready to return home—marking the first time Bangladesh has released a rescued tiger back into the wild.

The tiger, estimated at 10 years old, was found in the Chandpai and Sarankhola forest range of the Sundarbans East division. The mangrove forest, spanning 6,017 square kilometers across Bangladesh and India, is home to just 125 critically endangered Bengal tigers according to the 2024 census.

But preparing for her return has revealed a genuine dilemma. Experts disagree on whether to fit her with a satellite collar—a standard global practice for tracking released tigers—or rely on camera traps instead. Those favoring the collar argue it will help prevent her from wandering toward human settlements. Others worry the equipment and re-tranquilization could burden an already weakened animal.

The debate reflects something hopeful beneath the surface: the Forest Department's recent crackdowns on poachers are working. Over the past year, officials removed 35 kilometers of illegal snares and traps, detained 70 poachers, and the deer population has noticeably rebounded. One recent visitor even spotted female deer with triplets—rare in the forest for years.

This tiger's journey from snare to freedom represents progress hard-won.
